frames.name: Uncertain Succession Crisis
frames.description: Focuses on the unclear leadership transition and lack of organized opposition in Iran
frames.emphasis: The absence of credible democratic alternatives and the complexity of Iran's power structure making regime change difficult

frames.name: Strategic Military Success
frames.description: Presents the strikes as tactically successful in eliminating Iran's leadership succession bench
frames.emphasis: The operational effectiveness of eliminating multiple layers of Iranian leadership and constitutional succession mechanisms

frames.name: Historical Justice and Opportunity
frames.description: Frames the action as justified retaliation for regime brutality and an opportunity for Iranian liberation
frames.emphasis: The moral justification for the strikes based on Iran's crackdown on protesters and the potential for democratic transformation

frames.name: Regional Stability Concerns
frames.description: Emphasizes the risks of broader Middle East conflict and potential Iranian state collapse
frames.emphasis: The dangers of creating a failed state and escalating regional warfare

outlierPerspectives: Venezuela as a model for Iranian transition (mentioned only in The Atlantic)
Iran's constitutional succession mechanism being specifically targeted by strikes on the Assembly of Experts building (detailed only in Fox Politics)
The 'axis of aggressors' framing linking Iran to China, Russia, and North Korea (only in Foreign Policy)
missingContext: Detailed explanation of what triggered the immediate decision to strike now versus earlier opportunities
Analysis of Iran's actual military capabilities and likely retaliation scenarios
Assessment of regional allies' positions and potential responses
Examination of domestic Iranian public opinion beyond protest movements
Discussion of international law implications of targeting a sovereign nation's leadership
Analysis of how this action affects U.S. relationships with European allies and other partners
